cards.back: A special class member function that is executed when an object of that class is destroyed, designed to help clean up resources

cards.text:
Destructor syntax for class Simple: {{c1::~Simple()}} { /* cleanup code */ }
The destructor name is the class name preceded by a {{c2::tilde (~)}}.

cards.front: What are the key rules for destructors?
cards.back:
• Named with tilde (~) + class name
  • No parameters (cannot be overloaded)
  • No return type
  • Only one destructor per class
  • Cannot be called directly (called automatically)

cards.text:
Destructors are called automatically when:
- An object goes {{c1::out of scope normally}}
- A dynamically allocated object is {{c2::explicitly deleted using delete}}

cards.question: Why does this class need a destructor?
cards.answer: The class dynamically allocates memory in the constructor but never deletes it. Need destructor to delete[] m_array to prevent memory leak.
cards.explanation: When an IntArray object is destroyed, the m_array pointer is destroyed but the dynamically allocated array is not. A destructor must delete[] m_array.

cards.text:
Destructor to clean up dynamic array:
{{c1::~IntArray()}}
{
    {{c2::delete[] m_array;}}
}

cards.explanation:
A key C++ pattern where:
  • Resources (memory, files, locks) are acquired in the constructor
  • Resources are released in the destructor
  • Ensures automatic cleanup when object goes out of scope
  • Prevents resource leaks even with exceptions

cards.front: In what order are constructors and destructors called for multiple objects?
cards.back:
Constructors: called in order of creation (first created, first constructed)
Destructors: called in REVERSE order of creation (last created, first destroyed)

cards.question: When is the destructor called for this global object?
cards.answer: The destructor is called at program termination, after main() exits.
cards.explanation: Global objects are destroyed when the program ends. Local objects are destroyed when they go out of scope.

cards.front: What happens if you don't provide a destructor for a class?
cards.back: The compiler generates an implicit destructor that does nothing except call destructors of member objects. This is sufficient if the class doesn't manage resources.

cards.concept: When to write a custom destructor
cards.explanation:
Write a destructor when your class:
  • Dynamically allocates memory (new/new[])
  • Opens files or network connections
  • Acquires locks or other system resources
  • Needs to log destruction or update global state
Don't write one if the class only has simple member variables - compiler's implicit destructor is sufficient.

cards.question: What's wrong with this cleanup pattern?
cards.answer: The destructor is never called because the object was dynamically allocated but never deleted. This causes a memory leak.
cards.explanation: Destructors are only called automatically for stack objects going out of scope or when delete is explicitly called on heap objects.

cards.text: If you call {{c1::std::exit()}} or if the program crashes, destructors for {{c2::local/automatic objects}} are NOT called, causing potential resource leaks.

cards.front: Should you explicitly call destructors in normal code?
cards.back: No. Destructors are meant to be called automatically by the compiler. Explicitly calling destructors is rarely needed and can cause double-destruction bugs.

cards.text: Destructor characteristics: {{c1::no parameters}}, {{c2::no return type}}, {{c3::cannot be overloaded}}, {{c4::one per class}}
RAII pattern: acquire resources in the {{c1::constructor}}, release resources in the {{c2::destructor}}
Destructors are called in {{c1::reverse}} order of construction: last created object is {{c2::first}} destroyed.
